summary: I was born in Ellenville, New York, where my parents lived after moving from the Dominican Republic. My dad moved there for work as a master electrician, and later brought my mom there after they married. Growing up in Ellenville shaped a lot of my early life experiences before I joined the Navy in 1987.

My parents were originally from the Dominican Republic, and my grandfather served in the Dominican Army. My dad moved to Ellenville for work, then returned to the Dominican Republic to marry my mom before bringing her back to New York. The Dominican Republic is a big part of my family's history and heritage.

My grandfather served in the Dominican Army, which is the only military service in my family before me. He was part of the military back in the Dominican Republic, and that connection to service influenced my understanding of duty. It’s a proud part of my family’s background.

I joined the United States Navy right after high school in July 1987 because I wanted to get into aviation without going to college first. The Navy gave me the chance to train as an acoustic operator on the P-3 Orion and to fly without being a commissioned pilot. I went through boot camp in Orlando, training in Pensacola, Memphis, and Jacksonville, and served in various roles including logs and records and flying missions in the late 1980s.

Orlando, Florida was where I attended boot camp when I first joined the Navy in 1987. It was my introduction to military life, discipline, and leadership, where I earned the rank of starboard watch petty officer. Orlando marked the beginning of my Navy career and shaped my approach to service.

After boot camp in Orlando, I went to Pensacola, Florida for aircrew school where I learned the basics of aviation and flight operations. Pensacola was where I started specialized training for my role as an acoustic operator on the P-3 Orion. It was also a place where I and my friends would unwind on weekends, enjoying the boardwalk and local entertainment.

Near Memphis, in Millington, Tennessee, I received specialized training on the acoustic equipment used on the P-3 Orion. This was a key part of my preparation to operate the complex systems for submarine detection. The training there was technical and helped me become proficient in my Navy role.

Jacksonville was where I completed the final phase of my Navy job training, including classified instruction related to our missions. It was also where I started flying regularly on the P-3 Orion before joining my permanent squadron. The work there was intense and involved handling sensitive information.

The P-3 Orion was the aircraft I operated as an acoustic operator in the Navy during the late 1980s. We used it primarily for hunting Soviet submarines in the Mediterranean Sea, employing underwater sound acoustics and oceanography techniques. Flying the P-3 was a big part of my Navy career and involved both training and real reconnaissance missions.

During my Navy service in the late 1980s, the Soviet Union was sending many submarines into the Mediterranean, which we were tasked to track and monitor. Our missions on the P-3 Orion involved surveillance to ensure they stayed outside allied limits. The Cold War context made our work critical and highly focused on Soviet naval activity.

After my Navy service, I attended the University of Miami for two years where I initially went to play baseball. Unfortunately, I got injured and later transferred to Florida State University to finish my degree. The University of Miami was my first step into college after the military.

My older sister attended Vassar College, which is a private school like the University of Miami where I wanted to go. Because both of us would have been in private colleges at the same time, my parents couldn’t afford it, which influenced my decision to join the Navy to help with college costs. Vassar was part of my family’s educational background.

I transferred to Florida State University after my injury at the University of Miami and completed my degree there. I majored in multinational business operations with a minor in Spanish. Florida State was where I finished my college education after serving in the Navy.

Brunswick, Maine was the home base of my permanent Navy squadron during my service. From there, we deployed to places like Sicily for missions tracking Soviet submarines in the Mediterranean. Brunswick was where we trained extensively for various exercises including torpedo qualifications.

The Mediterranean Sea was a key operational area during my Navy service in the late 1980s. We flew P-3 Orion missions there to track Soviet submarines and conduct surveillance to protect allied interests. It was a tense environment due to the Cold War, and our work involved a lot of reconnaissance and war game exercises.

Although not detailed extensively, Saudi Arabia was mentioned as part of the broader region connected to some of our Navy operations or deployments. It was relevant to the geopolitical context of the late 1980s and early 1990s during my service. The region’s strategic importance was part of our mission planning.

Jeddah was referenced in relation to the Navy’s operational areas or ports during deployments. It was part of the Middle East region connected to our missions, especially during times of heightened military activity. Though I did not elaborate much, it was a location relevant to our Navy presence.

Marriott International came up in conversations about civilian careers after military service, particularly related to hospitality and management. While I didn’t work there myself, it was mentioned as an example of a company that hires veterans or is part of the broader job market. It represents the kind of civilian opportunities available post-service.

Embry-Riddle Aeronautical University was mentioned as one of the aviation schools I considered before joining the Navy. The Air Force required college education or aviation school like Embry-Riddle for pilots, which I did not want to pursue immediately. The Navy allowed me to enter aviation training straight out of high school without college first.

United Airlines was mentioned in the context of Navy pilots transitioning to commercial airlines after their military service. I worked in logs and records where pilots would request documentation of their qualifications to help with airline job applications. United was one of the major airlines that valued military flight experience.

Delta Air Lines was another major airline where Navy pilots often went after their military careers. In my role managing logs and records, I helped pilots document their qualifications for airlines like Delta. The airline industry highly regarded military-trained pilots for their skills and readiness.
